Now let's talk about how you take the building blocks of a great sales letter and you stack them, you put them together, so that they form a persuasive and powerful sales letter that makes you money. So here's what we're going to cover. We're going to cover what the building blocks really are, how do you stack them, and how this all works together with the pastor framework, because that's the question I get a lot. It's like, okay, how do I write a sales letter using pastor, what goes where? Well, this is how you do it. These first few blocks correspond with the P part of the pastor acronym, the person, the problem, and the pain, the way they experience it. So building block number one, this is the headline. Think of the headline as the ad for the rest of the ad, and you might say, I'm not writing an ad, of course you are. You're writing a page on your website, perhaps, that sells your product or service, or sells people on coming in for an interview or an evaluation. This is an ad, and you're trying to make a sale, even if the sale is merely for them to pick up the phone and give you a call. The headline's job is just to get their attention and get them to stop in their tracks and read whatever else you have on the page. That's the only job that headline has. Building block number two is your core thesis paragraph. So what's a core thesis? Well, it's very simple. This is the big idea that you're trying to convey in your copy. What's the big idea behind how you help people solve their problem? You need to be able to express this in a single crystal clear sentence. Building block number three is the lead. The lead is the beginning of your copy, the first sentence or paragraph, where you connect through the problem and the pain they feel in the way that they feel it and experience it. The next few building blocks in your sales copy correspond to the A and the S in the pastor framework. The section where we amplify the pain and the cost of not solving the problem and the story and solution to that problem and the end to the pain that they're feeling. Building block number four is just the body of your text, the bulk of everything you write. All the other elements that I'm about to tell you about are part of the body of the sales copy. Building block number five are the subheads. These are smaller headlines, and just like the main headline's job is to get you to read the ad. It's the ad for the rest of the ad. These smaller subheads have the job of getting people to read the different sections of your sales copy because they're not reading every word of the copy at first. There are three things that people never do at first when they read your sales page. The first thing they never do at first is read anything. The second thing they never do is believe anything, and the third thing they never do is buy anything. So you might say, well, what's the point? I might as well give up. No. Remember the words at first. We've got to capture their attention. So when they're skimming, scanning, and scrolling through your copy, the one thing you want them to see is the subheads that stand out throughout the body of the copy. In fact, when you put them together with the headline and then the subheads, you're leading them through the main points of your sales argument. So think of this as the shortened version of what you're trying to say to them in the copy. If they're skimming down the page, you want them to stop at some point and say, I need to go back and read this because this looks interesting. So think of the subheads as the bucket brigade of your copy. You know, in the old days, you would build a village by a lake or by a river, and you did that because you needed water to drink. And also, if there was a fire, people would line up and they would pass a bucket of water, one person to the next, from the body of water to the building that was on fire, and that's how they put out fires. That was the bucket brigade. These subheads are like the bucket brigade of your copy. They keep people moving from one section to the next. So now you've got them reading, and as they read, they will read your stories. They will read your testimonials, and they will begin to believe, which they didn't do at first. And of course, the ultimate outcome is we want them to buy. They don't do it at first, but they will eventually. So let's move on to the next building block. Building block number six is less a discreet piece of copy and more of a quality you want to build throughout your copy as you're constructing your sales page. This is rapport. This is how you connect with your reader. You demonstrate that you know them, that you understand their pain and their problem. And you discovered all of this when you created your Storybrand One page. So you already know this. You just need to find ways to work it into the copy as you're writing. Building block number seven are bullet points. These are brief statements that arouse curiosity about your product or service and how it solves their problem. And the reason it's important to use plenty of bullet points in your copy is because what do people never do at first? They never read it first. So we need to make sure that our copy aesthetically on the page has lots of white space, lots of subheads and lots of bullet points because all those elements are easier to skim, scan, and scroll, and you're more likely to capture their attention. These next two blocks correspond to the T in the Pastor Framework. And they are number eight, credibility. This is where we answer the question, why should I listen to this person? So this could be just your logos on the page that establish you as an expert or top person or company in your field. It can also be any credentials that you may want to share with your readers. And then building block number nine are the testimonials, the third party proof that your solution does what you claim. And again, it's very important that it comes from other people, not just from you, because people may or may not believe what you say about yourself, but they will believe what other people say about you. These next few building blocks correspond to the O in the Pastor Formula. This is the offer. And remember, this is one of the trickier parts of your copy. So you want to pay special attention to these building blocks and how you construct them. Building block number 10 is the implicit offer. This is the transformation. Remember that we're spending about 70 to 80% of our time in this section of copy on this very element, the transformation, the change, the new life that they're going to enjoy once they experience your solution to their problem. Building block number 11 is the price and the value justification. This is where you highlight the value of your offer and you want to contrast it favorably to the price. In other words, you want to show people why if they purchase your solution, it's actually worth more to them than the money they're exchanging it for. And if you can do this successfully, you'll have no problem making the sale. Building block number 12 is something called risk reversal. Now, this is the guarantee, the money back guarantee, which is language you need to use, but you need to go beyond this and let the reader know that you're taking the risk away from them and you're putting it back on yourself. So maybe if you're selling a digital product, you might say to them, look, I'm taking the risk because you could buy this from me, download all the material and keep it and ask for your money back. And I would have no choice but to give you your money back, but you would still have the product. So I'm the one taking the risk. Or you could say if you offer a professional service, if you're not satisfied with the service, I will refund your money, even though I've gone to all the time and trouble and expense of doing the work for you. So you're showing them that you have taken the risk upon yourself and taken it away from them. Now, you have to figure out how this applies to your own business or your own product or your own service that you're offering, but you can see how if you remove the fear, you remove the biggest obstacle to them making a purchase. Building block number 13. You may or may not always use this, but offering a bonus that is relevant to the product that you're selling and enhances the value is a powerful selling building block. This is an unexpected gift that enhances the value of your offer and it's that extra degree of effort, of excellence that makes people decide, yes, I'm going to get this. I'd be crazy not to. The final two building blocks fall under the R section of the pastor framework. That's the request to response section where you're asking for the sale. So building block number 14 is the explicit offer. Remember, we talked about the implicit offer before, which was the transformation that they're getting. The explicit offer is the call to action. This is where you ask for the order. You tell them exactly what they're getting and you tell them exactly what to do. And building block number 15 is the PS. Now, I know this seems silly in today's modern age. Why do we need a PS on a page on the internet? Because this came from the olden days when we actually wrote letters and we would get to the end of the letter and remember, we forgot something. So we would put a post script at the end of the letter, PS. We obviously don't need to do that in this day of word processors and web pages, but what happens when people encounter your web page, especially your sales pages, they scroll to the bottom and what are they looking for? What are you looking for when you scroll to the bottom of a page? The price! And we usually scroll all the way to the bottom. And if you have a PS at the bottom that summarizes the main benefit of your offer and links back to the offer section, you may have captured their attention before they bounce off the page and are gone forever. So I think using a PS is very important. And those are the building blocks of a sales letter using the pastor framework.
